system_prompt:
You are distilling one chapter of a guitar method book into a short summary.

The user will give you a chapter file with topic-organized verbatim quotes
plus the human-rationale notes for each. Write a one-paragraph summary (4-7
sentences) that captures:

  - what this chapter teaches (the techniques, concepts, rules, examples)
  - the master's characteristic moves visible in this chapter (if any)
  - the load-bearing terminology this chapter introduces

Reference the topic labels from the chapter file when useful. Do not invent
information not present in the chapter. If the chapter is biographical or
introductory rather than instructional, say so explicitly.

Output just the paragraph — no preamble, no JSON, no markdown formatting.
user_prompt:
Chapter 4: Displacement

<<<CHAPTER FILE
# Chapter 4 — Displacement

_Pages 37-44._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Displaced dominant chord approach methods

> There are several ways to approach displaced dominant chords. They are often paired
> with related II-7s, forming a ILV7 pattern. They may also be preceded by another
> dominant chord a perfect fifth or a half step higher. A displaced target chord may also
> be approached using any of the other techniques discussed throughout this book.

**Significance (p. 37).** Enumerates the core methods for approaching displaced dominant chords.

## Tritone substitution to fix melody/harmony clashes

> To fix the melody/harmony problem found in measure 1, beat 2, | do two things. | use
> tritone substitution fo change F#7 to C7, and adjust the chord quality to sus4 to account
> for the fourth in the melody.

**Significance (p. 39).** Two-step technique for resolving b9 clashes from displacement.

## Sus4 adjustment for melody/harmony clash

> To fix the melody/harmony problem found in measure 1, beat 4, | adjust the chord
> quality to sus4 fo account for the fourth in the melody.

**Significance (p. 39).** Rule for using sus4 quality to resolve a fourth-in-melody clash.

## Special case: minor seventh with b3 in lead displaced by half-step II-V7

> 1. A minor seventh chord with 3 in the lead may be displaced by a ILV7
> patiern a half step higher. The melody note that was originally >3 becomes
> tension 9 on the II-7 of the newly inserted pattern.

**Significance (p. 40).** Defines Special Case 1 with qualifying condition and tension outcome.

## Special case: minor seventh with b5 in lead displaced by half-step II-V7

> 2. A minor seventh chord with b5 in the lead may be displaced by a II-V7
> pattern a half step higher. The melody note that was originally b5 becomes
> tension 11 on the Il-7 of the newly inserted pattern.

**Significance (p. 40).** Defines Special Case 2 — completes the pair of special displacement rules.

## b3 displacement technique in practice

> In the reharmonization, each b3 melody note becomes a fension 9 over a new Il-V pattern.

**Significance (p. 41).** Restates the b3→T9 transformation rule as applied.

## b5 displacement technique — general applicability

> This technique works well wherever b5 is found in the lead of a minor 7(b5) chord. The
> melody note, which was originally b5, becomes fension 11 on the II-7 of the newly
> inserted pattern.

**Significance (p. 41).** Generalizes the b5 displacement rule as a portable technique.

## Cadential dominant as preferred displacement target

> A cadential dominant chord is offen a good choice as a displaced target chord.
> Dominant seventh chords have usually been carefully placed in order io establish the
> framework of the original phrase. Displacing these chords produces a noticeable change
> in the harmonic shape.

**Significance (p. 37).** Foundational rationale for choosing dominant sevenths as displacement targets.

## Using tritone substitution to address melody/harmony clashes

> Look closely at the melody/harmony relationships, and note where tritone
> substitution (dominant chords a fritone higher} could be used to fix melody/harmony
>
> clashes.

**Significance (p. 38).** Tritone substitution as remedy for melody/harmony clashes.

CHAPTER FILE>>>
